title: Writing Good Commits
subtitle: Messages your future self will thank you for

sections.content: A good commit message is a gift to your future self (and your teammates). It should explain **why** you made a change, not just what you changed. Clear messages make git's history actually useful.
sections.analogy: Writing good commit messages is like labeling photos on the back. '3rd birthday party at grandma's house' is helpful. 'photo' is not. Months later, those labels are the only way you'll remember what was happening in each snapshot.
keyPoints: Good messages explain the **why**: 'Fix login timeout for slow connections'
The first line should be short (under 50 characters) and in imperative mood: 'Add', 'Fix', 'Update'

sections.instruction: You have a blog project with HTML and CSS. Let's practice the full workflow with good commit messages.
sections.prompt: Type `git init` to start tracking this project.

sections.instruction: Stage all files for your initial commit.

onSuccess.message: All files staged. Now for the important part — writing a descriptive commit message.

sections.instruction: Your initial commit should describe what the project contains. Don't just write 'first commit' — be specific about what you're starting with.
sections.prompt: Write a descriptive commit. Try: `git commit -m "Set up blog with HTML structure and base styles"`
sections.hint: Type: git commit -m "Set up blog with HTML structure and base styles"

onSuccess.message: Great message! It describes WHAT the commit contains. Someone reading your git log would immediately know what this project started with.

sections.question: Which commit message follows best practices?
options: "Add user login validation for email format"
sections.explanation: Good commit messages are specific and start with an imperative verb: 'Add', 'Fix', 'Update'. They explain what and why, not just 'stuff' or 'changes'. Your future self will thank you.

sections.instruction: Now add a new feature to your blog — a navigation bar.
sections.prompt: Type `echo "<nav>Home | About | Contact</nav>" >> index.html` to add navigation.

onSuccess.message: Navigation added. Now stage and commit this change.

sections.prompt: Type `git add .` to stage your changes.

onSuccess.message: Staged. Now write a commit message that describes this specific change.

sections.instruction: Write a commit message for this change. Describe what you added, not just 'update'. Good examples: 'Add navigation bar to blog', 'Add nav links for Home, About, Contact'.
sections.prompt: Commit with a descriptive message using `git commit -m "..."`
sections.hint: Type: git commit -m "Add navigation bar to blog"

onSuccess.message: Each commit tells a story. When you run git log, you should be able to understand the project's history just by reading the messages. That's the mark of a professional developer.

completionMessage: Good commit messages are a superpower. They help your teammates, your future self, and anyone who reads your code. The rule: describe WHAT changed and WHY, not just THAT something changed.
